is an example of fine poetry.2 In this chapter alone he emp-
loys seven varieties of the Aryd and 52 other metres. He has used three
kinds of Danfakas. In this connection we can remember that Pingala
vneniion* Rdtam&ndavya (or Rdta and Mdn4avya ?) as a writer on
prosody; and Varalia names Man^avya. It is possible that Rdta and
Mdn^avya refer to one and the same writer; but according to Utpala
they are two persons as Rataman^avya and Mandavya, Another
feature of this section is that he gives the definition of some of the
metres. For example, in verses 50 and 51 he gives the rules for
Upagiti and Aryd; in 57 for ^loka and in 58 for treating the last short
syllable in a foot as long.
Our author uses many metres which are not found in any work
on prosody. The two verses, viz., 5 and 13, ofXLVII have not been
named, though they come under the Pankti group. He resorts also
to grafting of one metre on another and thereby creates a new
